CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Somo Old-Age Pension Committecs ought to be rofir
without pension. We have particulars of a very glaring
case in the neighbourhood of Carrick-on-Suir. A claim
for full pension for an old man living ab Ballindesaxt,
Carrick-on-Suir, who had been getting 3s. & week, was
made last November. This new claim was embodied
in a sworn affidavit which showed that the old {nun’s
income was only £10 o year.” Tt was forwarded in the
beginning of January to the Jocal Pensions Committee
_of the district of Clonea and Rathgormack, Carrick-on-
" Suir, and that able and energetic committee do not meet

soggtigxe&:ior.thme months. Perhaps, they are anxious

. inflicted on Boyle.

to spare the silver of the British Treasury? We would
like to have the names of the members of this model
and virile committee. The secretary, to whom the
claim was addressed, sent it back with a direction that
it should have been sent to the Pension Officer! ~ We
must confess that the differentiation between the sec.
of a Pension Committeo and the Pension Officer is be-
yond our poor comprehension. The claim anyway was
sent to the Pension Officer, whoever that commissioned
gentleman may be.  After a considerable delay, the
TPension Officer saw the claimant and simply asked him
why he wanted the full pension! We think we could
answer that question ourselves—Of course, because he
was entitled to it. So far as our information goes the
virile and energetic Old-Age Pensions Committee of the
district of Clonea and Rathgormack never discussed the
case. It is even said that since the Pension Officer’s
visit to the old man, the secretary and the committee
have gone to sleep, and whilst they are having their
snooze, the poor old man is waiting for a visit from some
gentleman who is to inspect his seven hungry statute
acres, his'one cow, his one pig, and ten hens. One might
think that this rich old man, with a valuation of 50s.
or so, was looking for his full pension from his Majesty’s
most gracious Pension Officer, or from the most leisured
gentlemen of the Pension Cominittee of the district of
Clonea and Rathgormack. Perhaps, this “*scurrilous’’
paragraph may disturb the slumber of the committee
and officials. If it doesn’t, we may have to apply a
mustard plaster.

We believe Boyle, in the County of Roscommon, is
quite famous for its testimonials to peelers, bank clerks
and other nation-builders. A bank clerk from the
bigoted, anti-Catholic Bank of Treland, a Mr. Tyner, is
being shifted on promotion from Boyle to Clonakilty,
and of course the hat is going round and subscriptions
are limited to £1 each. *‘ The uniform courtesy and
great anxiety to oblige ’’ of Mr. Tyner are referred to
in the begging circular that is being sent round on his
behalf.  The joint secs. and treasurers are Michael
Candon, of whom we have no information, and William
J. Robinson, LL.B., who, we hear, is a member of the
pernicious secret society of Freemasons, and is locally
Jknown as the British Lion, as he sings a lyric of thab
name. The heir to the Rockingham estate will shortly
be of age, and we hear that another testimonial will be

Tast week we directed attention to the two ‘‘per-
secuted "’ Protestants who promptly left their lodgings
in Manorhamilton, when the ownership passed from a
Protestant to o Catholic. Leitrim has very few non-
Catholics, and their * persecution’ is worth inquiring
into. A Leitrim reader, in the course of a letter to us,
soys:i—‘ There are very few non-Catholics in_the
county; yob to look ab the officials of Banks, Post
Offico, ofc., one would imagine the very opposite. T
shall try to explain as briefly as possible how the
bigotry works in Carick-on-Shannon i—

Northern Banking Co.—One Catholic (Clerk).

Provineial Bank—One Catholic (Clerk).

Post Office—One Catholic (Clerk).
The well-paid officials are all Protestants—managers,
postmaster, cte.

District Inspector, R.I.C.—Non-Catholic.

County Inspector, R.I.C.—Do.

Clerk of County Council—Do.

Clerk of Crown and Peace for County—Do.
Now, is this not religious intolerance? ™

v

¢ Our correspondent further says:—* There is scarcely
& position in Leirim worth holding but is in the hands
‘of Protestants. There is a/ rumour that the Clerk of the
Petty Sessions for Drumshambo has resigned, and I
believe it will be a non-Catholic who will succeed him
‘that is, two non-Catholics in succession! It is the
.Jocal magistrates who have this in théir power.’”

Our correspondent also complains of the apathy of
the people with regard to'supporting Irish manufacture.
In Carrick-on-Shaunon, he says, it is only with great
difficulty one can get articles of I manufacture.
Perhaps one of the readiest and best ways of shaking up
Leitrim would be to extend the circulation of the
LEADER in that county, and some of our local friends
might be able to do something in that divection without
unreasonable trouble.

A VISION OF PATRICK’'S NIGHT.

Scene.—A hall of Tara, with one of the ancient kings
sitting in state surrounded by the chief men of
Erin,

The Chief Bard tunes up his harp and sings :—

The harp that once through Tara’s halls
The soul of music shed,

TIts songs of old ab last recalls
From ages dim and dead.

Now wakes the pride of former days,
Ere pothouse rhymes came o’er,

When Ireland sang no Cockney lays,
Nor shoneens trod her shore.

Once more to chiefs and men of might
The harp of Tara swells,

Though many a music hall to-night
Its tale of ruin tells.

The Paddy up to date now longs
For British trash galore.

The Tivoli and Empire songs
Are what he does adore.

King.—Chieftains and warriors of the days of old,
Statesmen and bards, we sit again awhile
Upon this ancient seat of Ireland’s kings
To judge of matters not of antique time
But of this present age. We know the past,
The present and the intermediate times,
And clearly see how centuries have changed
This ancient isle of scholars and of saints,
And so with mind that knows the old and new,
We're here to-night to pass our judgment on
Some national offenders. Haul in the first
Impostor. :

(The: guards drag in a Celtic bard.)

C.B.—What pagan vision’s this
That breaks upon the windows of my soul ?
It must be Tirnanog. Art thou, oh king,
The fairy monarch ?

XKing.—What is the charge

. Against this man of moonshine ?

1 Chief.—Oh king, this long-haired wight

- Pretends to know how we did think of old,
Beyond all others living here to-day.
He writes insane and most pernicious stuff
Concocted out of foreign superstitions
And filth engendered in low alien minds,
And calls them voices of the Irish soul,
Deep truthful studies of the Papist Gael,

+ And other seuryy nonsense. b

THE LEADER.

Marox 9, 1012.

King.—I sentence him to take a pickaxe up
‘And work as hard as those he doth traduce
Ifor seven weary years.

C. B.—Oh king, have mercy on my tender dreams,
And spare them from the pickaxe.

King.—The pen may be more mighty than the sword
For moral movements and constructive schemes,
But in thy hand ’tis but a dirty toy.

Come, take him off to do some honest work.
Haul in the néxt.

(Iinter Bung, led in by the collar.)

Bung.—Oh king, have pity on an honest Bung,
And don’t endorse his license.

1 Chief.—This man, my liege, 3 <
Hath eontravened the laws in selling drinks
Intoxicant and bad in their excess.

Fé knavishly doth place his profits high -
Above the true advancement of this land.
e sacrifices nothing for the aims

That are the best for Ireland.

Bung.—Oh my license, my license!

King.—T sentence Bung
To feed and clothe the victims of his trade
TFor ten long years, and after that to see
That they become teetotallers.

Bung.—Down goes my drunkery.

I cannot feed an army (Bung is hauled off).

King.—Who's next for us to serve ?

(A tolerance-prover is dragged in.)

1 Chief.—Great king, this creature is an abject slave.

King.—T know it by his looks.

Why doth he tremble so? -

1 Chief.—Perhaps he thinks thou art a Protestant.

King.—Then T suspect this craven donkey is
A tolerance-prover.

1 Chief.—The same, my liege.

King.—This creature is incurable, and so
I sentence the forlorn wretch to be
A flunkey and a scullion evermore
Unto the vilest bigot that exists
Among the “ saved ” in Ireland.

(The Tolerance-prover is bundled out.)

1 Chief—There is another, a Sinn Feiner here.

King.—One made in Germany.

Go pack him off to there.

Marcr 9, 1912,

The English Sufiragettes had an evening out in Lon-
don, and the damage to property was probably much
greater than that accomplished by those of the Dublin
underworld who broke out and smashed windows last
August.  We don’t think that these hysterical females
will do~ any service to the cause which they profess to
champion. However, their's is an Iinglish question,
end we are not interested in England. ~ It is worthy
of note that our imitation Suffragettes in this island
do not imitate their Saxon sisters in window breaking
and gaol-going.  After all imitation is only Brumma-
gem, and is not the real thing. The window smashing
of the English Suffragettes will add to the growing dis-
gust entertained in Ireland for their flabby and ridicu-
lou§ imitators in Ireland. We think we are safe in
saying that the imitation Suffragettes are utterly ro-
pudiated by the women of Ireland. If certain classes
of Irish women want a vote in Ireland, their claim will
be considered when we have Home Rule.  TFor our
part we think it undesivable that Irishwomen and girls
should work in factories; but if it is a necessity that
some of them do so—when they are dragged from their
homes and have to fight for a living as wage-earners in
the industrial world—then their claim to have a vote is
one that we could not argue against. If women in
politics are somewhat out of place, women in factories
are somewhat out of place also; and if they have to go
to factories we see no reason why they should not have
the Parliamentary vote. ~When we have Home Rule
the matter of votes for women as an Irish question will,
we are confident, be attended to with sympathy and
reason, and probably if there were no notoriety hunters
shouting ¢‘ Votes for women '’ in strident voices, the
question would be dealt with. Some of the ridiculous
imitation Suffragettes in this country appear to think
they can hen-peck the country—that is a tougher job
than they may have been led to believe.

An English M.P. by the name of Ian Malcolm, the
member for Croydon, made a speech on February 29th,
at some Conservative association, the ‘* Glasgow
Herald ”’ report of which is before us. Mr. Malcolm,
M.P., was the principal speaker. What a comment it
is on our ‘‘ tolerance provers '’ that, notwithstanding
all their ¢ tolerance proving,”’ the flood of lies abowg
‘“ persecution *’ in Ireland goes on unabated. Ignorant
** tames’’ all over the country apparently feel—we can-
not say think, for we fear that is the last thing they
would attempt to do in this connection—that by ‘* prov-
ing their tolerance’ they are affecting the hearts
and consciences of the Unionists in Ireland and in Great
Britain. The more they ‘‘ prove their tolerance ’’ the
more their enemies will despise them and kick them.
Notwithstanding all our  tolerance proving,’’ in face
of the facts of anti-Catholic bigotry in Government
offices, railways, banks, insurance offices and in varioqs
other spheres, here is what Mr. Malcolm, M.P., tells his
Scotch audience :—

THE LEADER. g

““ What had they (Irish Nationalists) ever done
for commerce and for science? They had neglected
these, and had lived on ﬁhei brains ofﬂ?chers :1;
their lives—(applause)—yet these were the men
whom Mu. f\s}iﬁlith i)n g]old blood asked them to
hand over the lives and the fortunes of their co-
religionists and fellow-loyalists in Ireland. It
might be said that Mr. Redmond was quite willing
to give safeguards for the welfare and protection of
minorities, but if he was in a position to do that
when the Home Rule Bill was passed, gurely he
was in a position to do it now and save their loyalist
friends in the South and West of Ireland from
what he (Mr. Malcolm) could only describe as a
hell upon earth.”

That is all the ‘“tames” get for their ‘‘ tolerance
proving "’—the Nationalist outcasts are coolly told that
they *“ lived on the brains of others all their lives.”” We
suppose this in part means that the Catholic customers
of the Provincial Bank are living on the brains of that
bank’s staff !

The particular reader of ours who sent us this cutting
malkes the following remarks in the course of his letter:

I send you a tit-bit for the ‘ tolerance provers.”
What do you think of it? It is a bit of grim ivony
that, whilst Mr. Malcolm is weaving airy fictions
about S. and W. Treland, the Orange members of
Motherwell School Board have been doing their
best to get a teacher to resign because—in the exer-
cise of liberty of conscience—she has become a
Catholic. As the young lady will not resign, the
intention is to dismiss her, and a resolution to that
effect will be moved at the next Board meeting.””

In the issue dated February 17th, of the great American
Catholic weekly review, ‘“ America,’’ there is a leading
article on Monsignor O'Riordan’s powerful and brilliant
article in a recent issue of the LEaprr. The article is
headed ‘‘The Motu Proprio in Ireland.”” It in-
cidentally refers to * tolerance proving,” and it will
interest our readers to know the view a high-class
American Catholic review takes of that weakness. We
malke no apology for quoting the article in full:—

““ Dr. O’Riordan, Rector of the Irish College in
Rome, and author of ‘ Catholicity and Progress in
Ireland,” has a luminous article in the Dublin
LEADER, January 27, on the recent Motu Proprio,
which gave such trouble to some Irish Protestants.
On the supposition that it applies to Treland, which
he thinks improbable, he turns the tables neatly
on its principal denouncers. These arve Sir Edward
Carson and Mr. Campbell, K.C., both members for
Trinity College, Dublin.  Mr. Campbell called it
‘ a deadly blow at the sanctity and security of pro-
perty,” because it forbids Catholics, under pain of

excommunication, to cite their clergy before the
secular courts without permission of their bishops.
Dr. O'Riordan shows that a similar rule exists in the
Church’ of England, among the Scotch Presby-
terians and the Wesleyans, and in most clubs and
societies; but its most exact pavallel is found in
the chartered laws of Trinity College. The rule is
also in Latin, and reads thus: * All domestic dif-
ferences shall be examined and, if possible, decided
within the College. He who brings another into
Court without the consent of the Provost and the
majority of the Senior Fellows, shall be expelled
from the College.’

“In fact, the rule of Trinity, which Sir Iidward
Carson and Mr. Campbell represent, is much more
severe than the Papal Decree which they denounce.
The Catholic complainant has only to ask the per-
mission of one bishop, who is enjoined to grant it;
but the Trinity litigant has to obtain the consent
of the majority of the Fellows, as well as of the
Provost, and has no assurance that he will get it.
‘ Yet,” says Dr. O'Riordan, ‘ Irish Protestants have
been so born into privileges and so long and ex-
clusively enjoyed them that men of position, like
Mr. Campbell, can speak without shame, in a
Catholic city and country, of a solemn act of the
Pope, as ‘"an arrogant and insolent Decree.’’
They have been so long accustomed so to think and
speak, that they are regardless or unconscious of
their offensiveness.  If the Catholics of Dublin
held a meeting and resolved: ‘‘ Since the Motu
Proprio affects Catholics only, we request him and
his friends to mind their own business,’” Mr. Camp-
bell would probably be surprised at their developed
audacity. Such has been the fruit of Protestant
privilege in Ireland, they cannot reconcile them-
selves to the change which a century has made.
Hence, what is of divine right in a Protestant, in
the Pope or in a Papist is that sin ‘‘ which shall
not be forgiven.’’’

‘“The point is well taken. Continued Protes-
tant Ascendancy in the British Isles, and in not a
few places outside of them, had lef an air of in-
solent dominance on the one hand and servile
timidity on the other, long after the laws that en-
forced them had become obsolete. Dr. O'Riordan’s
famous reply to Horace Plunkett’s patronizing
criticism of the Catholic Church in Ireland, and
the LEADER, the organ in whick it first appeared,
have done much to awaken the virility of Irish
Catholics and put the party of ascendancy in its
place; but even yet there is too much talk of
tolerance and intolerance. There are Catholics,
not all of them in Ireland, who are cager to prove
their tolerance by giving their enemies everything
good that is going. Under free institutions there
should be no question of ‘ tolerance,” but of justice
and equality of opportunity. Catholics, as well as
others, have a right to claim and exact what their
inerit,s as citizens entitle them to, not more and not
ess.’

There was a meeting recently of the North Kildare
Branch of the Women’s Unionist Association, at which
3 Mr. Clements presided! There appears to have been
comparatively quite a large number of males at this
small anti-Irish hen party. A Sir Irederick Shaw
spoke and referred to Mr. Churchill having gone to
Belfast * protected by five thousand bayonets and one
petticoat.”” We take it that the latter had reference to
the fact that Mrs. Churchill accompanied her husband,

and we must say that Sir Frederick Shaw musb be some-
thing of a cad, for it was a very caddish renark. A
rev. gentleman named Fletcher, not Du(_llcy this time,
but Lionel, held forth on the anti-Catholic (;wkeg;. He
wanted to know who was to rule Ireland, was it Pope
Pius X. or King George V.? Perhaps, Lionel would
step into the breach and rule us himself!  The Rev.
Lionel went on about ‘“ Ne Temere ” and the recent
““ Motu Proprio,”” two Decrees that emanated from a
foreign potentate.”  This Mr. Fletcher IL. had his
anti-Catholic say, and then the chairman deprecated
bringing in anti-Catholic bigotry, and he hopcdAth‘ey
would have a large number of Catholics in the Unionist
Alliance.  But what is the anti-Flome Rule case but
one serecch against tho Catholic religion?

St. Patrick’s Day processions in other parts of the
country might consider adding such a feature to their
parades.

The Belfast Corporation issued a return of the num-
ber of Catholics and non-Catholics in their employment
on salary and the money paid. According to this
return in the City of Belfast, where the Cabhohcs'are
one-fourth—or, to be precise, 24.1—-of the population,
there are 604 non-Catholics getting £75,642 4s.,2d.,
whilst there are only 100 Catholics, and they only get
£4,230 3s. 10d. ; the average non-Catholic salary is £125
4s. 0d., whilst the average Catholic salary is only £64
5s.8d. Those who made the return did all they could
to make out the Catholic side as big as possible, but
notwithstanding they could only show that the Catho-
lics got in salaries £4,230 as against £75,642 to the non-
Catholics; or, in a city where they are about three-
fourths of the population, the non-Catholics receive
salaries from the Corporation which total between
seventeen and eighteen times as much as the Catholicsd
But that is on the Corporation’s own return. Even that
return was juggled and cooked. Councillor P. Dempsey,
of Belfast, maintained that there were only fifteen
Catholic employees who could be legitimately termed
salaried officials in the employment of the Belfast Cor-
poration. It appears the 'slim compiler, or compilers,
of this return hunted round in (he Library
and Technical Instruction Department, the Asylum
and the Fever Hospital, for Catholics, with a view to
putting the best possible face on the return. Some of
the persons included had as low as £5 a year as'sessional
teachers: even a kitchen maid was put down as a sala-
ried official of the Belfast Corporation; and nurses, at
about £10 a year in the Fever Hospital, were included
in the list of salaried persons under the Corporation.
This grotesque salaries” return has been sent back for
alterations and repairs, and the public will be interested
in the revised return.

The attempt of the Tory Post Office Permanent Offi-
cials in London to place difficulties in the way of the
transfer of the Irish Post Office under Home Rule is
attracting widespread attention amongst Irishmen in
Ingland as well as in Treland. The Irish Stores Depart-
ment is now contyolled from London, and three of the
four principal appointments in Dublin are filled b
importations. Xyvery man sent from London to Dublin
means the promotion of several men in London, whilst
it means stifling promotion amongst the staff in Dublin.
Not satisfied with dumping clerks over here, the Tory
officials in London now fhreaten to dump porters over

sure of Boy Messengers? The Irish Trades Union
ought to look into this matter. The officials whom it
is proposed to dump here-from Iingland do not, we
understand, like the prospect at all, and those men
already dumped are discontented ; needless to say the
Irish officials are very discontented at thoe stifling of
promotion so far as they ave concerned. The London
officials are sent over here on a lower scale of pay than
}'ules in London, whilst the cost of living here for them

Apparently, with the view to prevent the restoration
of the Irish Stores Branch to the Irish Sccretary, the
Tory officials in London have degraded Dublin {o the
rank of a sub-office under Birmingham, and this is only
the first move in the plot. Over £1,300 of public
money has been spent sending over officials to count a
few gross of pencils, a few quires of paper and the like ;
these trips are nice holiday excursions for the Tnglish
trippers for a stocktaking which was done by the men
on the spot until the amalgamation in 1908. What
is Mr. Samuel, the Liberal Home Ruler, doing that he
is allowing his Tory Permanent officials to carry on
these little games?  And what are the Irish M.P.’s
doing? The Irish M.P.’s are waiting for Home Rule,
but that is no reason why they should allow Unionist
Permanent officials to work away like this; indeed, the
near prospect of Home Raule is all the more reason why
they should be active to see that the problems of trans-
ferring the Irish Government to the Irish Parliament
are not made any more difficult than a century of Ascen-
dancy and anti-Irish jobbery have already made them.

Five years ago, when the Irish Stores Branch was
withdrawn, advantage was taken of the fact that Eger-
ton, the Secretary in Ireland, is an invertebrate, and
fair. promises were made to the Dublin staff.  These
promises were not kept, and probably it was the inten-
tion of the permanent officials that they would not be
kept. It was also promised that the great bulk of the
stores required in Ireland would be purchased in Ire-
land. This promise was thrown out to avert criticism,
and we fear it was never intended to be redeemed. Ad-
vantage was also taken by the Permanent Tory officials
of the then P.M.G.’s—Sydney Buxton—inexperience
and ignorance of Post Office administration. The pro-
tests of the Irish Members were unheeded; probably
they did not show enough fight. The ease with which
the change was made, and the weakness of Egerton
since then, have encouraged the London officials to
entirely disregard Irish interests. But let them take
eare, for Irish interests are not to be disregarded with
impunity.

Our readers will be glad to learn that the Belfast
Annual Irish Religious Celebration of Féile Pédruig,
which has been conducted with so much distinctiveness
and success in that city during the past six years, will
be continued this year. Hitherto the event has taken
place in St. Patrick’s Church, but owing to Confirma-
tion and Mission services in the latter church on the
17th inst.; it has been decided to hold the Irish religious
celebration of this year in the Sacred Heart Church,
Belfast. In this latter church the Children’s Mass has,
we learn, been sung in Irish every Sunday during the
past five years. The pavish priest, Rev. J. K. O’Neill,
chairman of the Committee of Coldiste Chomhghaill,
has, for upwards of twenty years, been an energetic
supporter of the Irish Language movement. The
forthcoming service will be held at 7 p.m. on the Feast
of the National Apostle, and the sermon will be preached
by the Rev. James Clenaghan, B.A., B.D., Catholic
Dean of the Queen’s University. As usual, the students
of St. Mary’s Training College, of St. Malachy’s Col-
lege, and of the Christian Brothers will assist in the
Trish hymns and responses.

Dear Sir,—Your correspondent, ‘‘ Observer,” does
not confute a solitary statement in my letter appearing
in your issue of 24th February. I thank him for the

ferent firm of gas engineers at Westminster than that
alluded to in your paper of April 1st, 1911.”"

The firm I allude to is one known as Anderson Bros.,
of 5 Westminster Chambers, London. I know of no
firm named the ‘ Anderson Gas Trust,”’ but I assume
this may be some term he has got for the firm I men-
tion, and only goes to show that his information on
other subjects may be equally as unreliable.

‘“ Observer ” speaks of making fullest enquiries ‘‘from
a large gas consumer and business man in Waterford
ivho knows something of the inner working of this con-
cern,”’ as if to infer that there is something dark and
shady about the transactions of this firm. The firm of
Anderson Bros. conduct their business, both ‘“inner ”’
and ‘‘ outer,”’ as thorough, honest business men, and
are in no way ashamed to let the light of day or of
unbiased criticism fall on their doings; and I repeat
that every assertion I made in my letter appearing on
the 24th ult: is true, and Fcan vouch for same by giving
names and data, and I again reiterate that no Irish in-
dividual, firm, town council or corporation, has done
more for Irishmen in the gas industry than has the
firm of Anderson Bros.

THE INSURANCE ACT IN THE RURAL
DISTRICTS.

N my former articles I have dealt with the effect of
the Insurance Act on the urban population. - In
this one I intend to treat of those of its provisions which
have a more particular bearing on the inhabitants of
the country districts. Naturally, where the conditions
of life are so different, the effects of any measure will
also be different, but though the Insurance Act may not
be so productive of good in the rural districts as it pro-
mises to be in the urban, yet some substantial benefits
will, I think, accrue from it for our rural population.

Of the two classes of Insured Contributers under the
Act, the Voluntary Contributor will, in the country dis-
tricts, be recruited from the small farmers, the small
shopkeepers, and the local artizans, including the vil-
lage smith or carpenter, none of whose incomes must
exceed. £160 per annum. They will, as in the case of the
urban Voluntary Contributor, have to pay the full Em-
ployed Contribution, which includes not only the Em-
ployee’s Contribution, but also the Employer’s, and
works out at 53d. a week for male Voluntary Contri-
butors, and 4%d. for female.

In view of the low sickness rato in country districts,
and the relatively high demands on Voluntary Contri-
butors, it is doubtful whether it will be worth most
farmers’ or shopkeepers’ while to becomo insured, espe-
cially as the value of the benefits to be received would
vary inversely to the age of the insured, should he have
passed forty-five on entering.  As it is Yo married men
Insurance would chiefly appeal, and as most of these
men would, in consequence of the late age at which
men marry in rural Ireland, be over forty-five, the in-
ducements held out to the rural Voluntary Contributor
are not very great.

The Labourers will constitute the largest section ot
the Compulsorily Insured in the country districts, bub
their ranks will be swelled by Dome Servants, male
and female, including priests’ “‘ boys,”” and by such
artizans as assistant smiths, carpenterd, ole., working
for a master smith ot carpenter, and presumably also by
the junior assistant masters and mistresses in the
Wational Schools, who have no claim to Pension or Dis-
ablement Allowance, and who have, moreover, no
security of tenure in their employment.

The most important exemptions from Insurance are
(1) sons or daughters working on their parents’ farm
will not be insured, even should they be paid a regular
fixed wage; and (2) any person working on an agricul-
tural holding who is not paid a money wage will not be
insured, even though there may be a definite arrange-
ment that such person must give a certain number of
days’ labour per week, month, or year, as the case may
be, in return for free use of a house or land, or as pay-
ment for some other privilege granted to him by the
owner or holder of the land.

Again, those harvesters who, in accordance with a
custom very prevalent in the West, spend the harvest-
ing season in Great Britain, and return to their homes
in Ireland for the rest of the year, are entitled to
exemption provided they are not, when at home, em-
ployed within the meaning of the Act; this does not pre-
vent them from working on a small holding of their
own, but only forbids their working for an
employer—and provided also that they spend at least
twenty-six weeks of the year at their home in TIreland.
It should be understood, however, that these people
are not excluded altogether from Compulsory Insur-
ance, but only that they have a right to exemption if
they wish to exercise that right and escape the contribu-
tions. They will, then, of course, lose all right to
benefits, but whether they claim exemption or not, their
British employers will have to pay the full (British)
employers’ rate of, at least, 3d. a weel, in respect of
them. This money, in the case of exempt labourers,
will be handed over to_ the Irish Insurance Commis-
sioners, and dealt with as they may divect. In the

unlikely case of a migratory labourcr’s not exercising
his right to-exemption, or if he has no such right, owing
to his being in fairly regular employment when at home
in Ireland, the monies paid by himself and by his em-
ployer in respect of him during the time he is ip Great

Britain, will also, presumably, be transferred to the °

Trish funds, though, in this case, ib.is to be supposed
that they will be put to his own credit in whatever
approved society, Post Office or other fund he is con-
nected with. 1

of the compulsorily insured. workers—I have already
dealt with the voluntary contributors—in country dis-
tricts, is rather a difficult one, because in the majority
of cases, the mere money wage is supplemented by the

vietim become permanently unable to work, ho will be
entitled to the regular Disablement Benefit of 5s. a
week, and this may be given, at the option of his socicty,
even though he may not be completely incapacitated.

Maternity Benefit will be, if anything, even more wel-
come in the country than in the towns, for the occasions
of receiving it occur with even greater regularity, and
there is no time when the possession of a little ready
Money is more acceptable.

In the case of insured contributors under 21 years of
age, though they do not get the benefit of the reduced
eontribution when the wages fall below a certain sum,
as stated above, they, nevertheless, are penalised in
specific cases. If they are unmarried and have no
one dependent on them, their sick pay is reduced from
10s. a week for 26 weeks to 6s. a week during the first

.13 weeks and 5s. during the second 13 weeks for men,

and from 7s. 6d. a week for 26 weeks to 5s. for the first
13 weeks and 4s. a week for the second 18 weeks for
women, and the Disablement Benefit is reduced, for
women only, from 5s. a week to 4s.

Owing to the big drain on our rural population caused
by emigration, the question must naturally arise as to
how intending emigrants will fare under the Act. Any
person who, at the time of emigrating, has been an in-
sured contributor for five years or over, may, if he is
going to a country where there is a similar system of
Insurance in force, be transferred to a society in his new
home, which will give approximately the same benefits
as he would have been entitled to, had he remained in
TIreland, but should no such society. exist, or should he
have been insured for any period less than five years,
he loses all benefit from the contributions paid by, and
in respect of, him during the years he has been insured.

of coal. To be strictly accurate, we should say
rather, to order a ton. It is for most of us not easy
to buy anything, except, perhaps, drinks and tobacco.
Everything else is bought with drops of blood, so to
spea The idle rich, the millionaires, of course, have
no difficulty in buying anything—except telegrams.
Telegrams are sold by the State, and this reflection
brings us abruptly to the subject we desire to discuss.
The State, so far, does not trade extensively. For
the most part it vends only such uninteresting wares
as stamps and postal orders. These, moreover, it sells
for spot cash. In the era before us there is a prospect
of the State becoming a gigantic coal merchant. The
purchasing of a ton of coal will then be a complicated
transaction. We in Ireland have had a foretaste of it.
In Ireland the State has been in trade for quite a long
time. Rude persons say that justice and high place
are sold for cash. That point we shall not consider,
but a regular trade has indubitably existed for many
years in cattle, boats, manures, and spraying machines.
In the light of experience, then, we may consider the
conditions in which coal will be bought when it becomes
the property of the State.

The purchasing of coal at ‘present is, as we have
observed, perfectly simple. Just as you are revolving
whether to pay your tailor or to indulge in the luxury
of a week-end, the powers of darkness inform you that
the coal is ““ out.”” ~ You murmur a prayer, look up the
last application for payment from the merchant to
whom you owe least, and then present yourself at his
office. This is some palatial edifice in a central tho-
roughfare. The counter and other wood-fittings are of
mahogany, highly polished. A magnificent fire burns
brightly in a burnished grate. Home, somehow, is
never like this. When you appear, an urbane youth,
dressed in the latest fashion, hastens to inquire your
wants. You state casually that you wish to ** settle '
an account, and affability merges into obsequiousness.
These preliminaries over, just as you are about to de-
part, you say, still more casually, ** Oh, by the way,
send me up a ton of coal, will you?”” 'To inquiries
whether you have a bias for ** Orrell ” or ** Kitchen,’’
you exclaim, still casually, * Oh-ah-ch, Kitchen will
do,”” and the transaction ends.

Consider how this piece of plain dealing will be
altered when His Majesty’s Government replaces the
coal merchant. We will continue to assume that you
never pay till to-morrow what you can owe to-day.
Once again you want coal. Acquainted with. the tape-
bound rules of Government Departments, you make
application in due form, in writing, intimating willing-
ness to pay for what you have had. You state, of
course, your name, address, date of birth, calling, and
nearest railway station. Having gratified official curi-
osity as to number in family, religion, and other per-
sonal matters, you ask to be supplied with a ton of coal
at current rates. Before a week has expired—such is
‘the express speed of Government Departments—you
receive a printed communication to the following
effect :—

As the pinch of cold is by this time beginning to nip,

You wend your way to a city slum and enter the dingy
portals of a derelict dwelling. In the hall a gentleman
at leisure directs your attention with a jerk of his head
to -the legénd ‘“Fuel Office’”” over a door, and you
make bold to go in.. Within is a counter much stained
with ink and painted to look like oak. Beyond this,
grouped round a roaring fire—no doubt an advertise-
ment—are several young men, also roaring. At first you
fear lest State secrets may reach your unprivileged
ears, but presently you gather that football is the
theme of the discussion.  The respective merits of
rival teams are neglected for the moment, arid a youth
whose antique coat is as that of Joseph—ink of many
colours having blended harmoniously with the original
dye—demands an explanation of your intrusion.

I T is now séveral months since the question of a boys’

paper was first seriously considered. More than
one thoroughly representative meeting took place, and
the undertaking gave grounds for considerable hope.
But: little has yet been done apparently, and in con-
sequence some young enthusiasts have cooled, some are
in despair, and some smile triumphantly to suggest that
they knew all along how it would be. The more level-

THE LEADER. 87

headed, however, will, perhaps, congratulate them-
selves that matters have not been rushed. The project
is not dead ; neither is it sleeping.  But in so grave and
serious an undertaking, hasty, immature action is more
to be deprecated than inaction. If a paper is to come
into existence, let it be one pure-blooded and vigorous
enough, not merely to survive its birth, but to live on
unto a ripe and honourable old age. 3

Tor that reason it is to be hoped that the matter will
be discussed from every point of view, aired and l)roug!!ﬁ
under the notice of all likely to be interested. — This
will aid the committee, which at present is working, to
bring about the realization of the project.

Is there, then, in the first place, a need of such a
paper? I have heard it said that there is, after all, no
greab need; that the boys’ papers already in existence
leave little to be desired; that the b story is in-
variably the most moral of all stories, for virtue
triumphs at least in the last chapter. Iven if these
statements were true, one would still have an economic
argument in favour of an Irish boys’ paper: for it is a
pity that our lads should have to depend for their read-
ing, as they have for their cricket bats, on foreigners.
But waiving this point, I am satisfied that neither from
the view-point of Irish-Ireland nor from that of the
Faith are these papers desirable reading for our boys.
I admit T have not read the boys’ papers regularly, but
those I have read were collected from the boys them-
selves. They are, therefore, read by boys, and are to
be taken as samples. I have besides a strong suspicion
that if one sets oneself seriously to the task, a very
much stronger case could be made out against them.
The first story read told of a pugilistic encounter
between a British sailor and a soldier, both branches
of the service that defends *“ our’’ Empire, coming in
for an ample share of praise. The “‘ gals,”” oh! espe-
cially the ‘‘ gals,”” and the canteen had eight or nine
references in the first four pages, showing what a jolly
time the hero and his comrades were having in the
army. He had left a sweetheart in China, a couple of
them in India, 4 few in South Africa, another in Malta,
and so on.  Hardly elevating reading this for plastic
young Irish minds, to say the least of it. ~But there
was worse.

In the next story the reader was introduced to a band
of robbers. In contrast with one of their number, a
contemptible character who, while thieving like the rest,
is all the time a spy, treacherously betraying his com-
rades to the police, the thieves become heroes, win the
warm sympathy of the reader and his intense desire for
their escape.

In a third there is a sketch of a lady detective, who,
to judge by the wood-cut, seems in all her adventures to
move abouf decolleté and to take up attitudes likely to
fascinate morbid readers.  She steals from the rich,
but feels quite justified as she lets the reader know she
will bestow it on others who are worse off.

A fourth story tells how the hero, a detective, saves

ing unsavoury details of her fall. Tho last of my read-
ings, at the advice of a colleague, I omit. 1t is.too

Some one will say these are the worst of the boys’
papers. Perhaps, that is so. They are, however, read
by boys, a sufficiently lamentable consideration.

But even the better papers leave much to be desired.
The ““ Boys’ Own Paper ’—which, by the way, is a
publication of the Protestant Tract Socicty—is on the
whole a clean, healthy paper, yet not long since T read in
it a story of a lad, rescued from the guardianship of a
drunken, superstitious, thieving, ne’er-do-well Irish-
man. The boy finds salvation in a quiet, Protestant,
English home.

The Guy Fawkes and the Spanish Armada story at
intervals atford opportunities for showing up the general
despicableness (?) of Catholic Spain and its heartless,
priest-ridden Inquisition. *‘ The Scout ” is also an ex
cellent paper so far as it goes, but it is British, Imperial
and military in its sentiments and aim. Besides, there
is this objection to all these papers that they set up the
very inadequate standard of mere ‘‘ honour’ as the
sufficient test and guide of conduct.

These are a few of the tares I have plucked at random
from a wide field. Surely they justify one in putting
the question: Can anything be done to remedy the evil?
1t is too much to hope that we can wipe out the Eng-
lish papers, perhaps.  But is it impossible to produce
a real, live, boys’ paper that will leaven our lads with
truer and higher ideals; one whose influence will be felt
as the influence of the LEADER itself is felt, out beyond
the circle of its readers?

That depends on the answer to two other questions.
Is there sufficient talent in the country to produce a
satisfactory paper? And, in the second place, are the
people of means in the country prepared to give the
project the financial assistance it deserves and needs?

And, first, what is a satisfactory paper?

All will agree that, if it is to succeed, it must be
such as will please the boys themselves.

Tt is of no avail to produce a good, pious paper that,
sailing under some semi-religious title, will be pur-
chased by the parents as a duty and read by their sons
as a penance. What, then, do the boys want?

They want, chiefly and above all, stories, and
amongst stories the most popular by long odds, so far
as I can hear, and T have asked the boys themselves, i8
the school story with its boxing matches, football or
ericket contest, its flavour of slang, elc., beaded along
4he thread of some manly lad’s history. ~ Next to the
school story comes sea-adventure, Red Indian stories,
cow-boy escapades, etc. Somo one will say, what is
Trish about all this? I answer it is for the author to see
that it has the Irish tinge, and I believe the tinge to
gerve its purpose best should be faint and subduqd, The
Trish element could be made strong to aggressivencss,
however, in the historical tale: There young Owen Roe
or some page-boy of Brian Boru might save the im-
perilled destiny of his country or his chief; while the
Saxon and the Dano might be painted in darkest hues
of treachery and cowardice and villainy.  Many boys
like essays on certain more serious subjects, if chattily

rFHIRTEEN thousand miles, with only three stops

on the way. Such is the passage from England
to New Zealand, via Cape Town. _Many people think
it a very monotonous journey. From Cape Town to
Tasmania, no land is seen for three weeks—often a cold,
stormy, uncomfortable three weeks. They say it can
be as rough around, and south, of the Cape as anywhere
on the wide seas.

Travel is now such an every-day affair that it seems
as uncalled-for to write about a sea voyage as aboub
one’s breakfast—especially if one has no uncommon ex-
periences to relate. I am not, therefore, going to de-
scribe our passage, beyond giving a few impressions by
the way.

Our very first act on boarding the New Zealand Steam-
ship’s fine boat of 11,500 tons, the *“ Rotorua,’” at Ply-
mouth, was to hold & political meeting! It was not
that we gathered the passengers together to talk Home
Rule to them, but that our Plymouth friends had come
out on the tender in goodly numbers, and met in the
smole-room to wish us good luck and good-bye. Shortly
afterwards, with the cheers of the Irish contingen$
dying away in the distance, we had started on our long
journey. .

With aecommodation for over eighty, there were only
ten first-class passengers, fifty or sixty second, and &
large number of third-class passengers. I was in-
terested to note the accommodation provided for the
latter, and so far as T could sece, it was in all respects
admirable. I travelled home from South America in
1910 on tho ‘* Princepessa Mafalda''—the latest, fast-
est, and finest of the Italian Royal Mail Steamers.
Though the highest of the four classes on that boat is
almosb extravagantly luxurious, the accommodation for
the emigrants is very poor, indeed, compared with the

properly cared for on their journey. - This system might
be worth adopting for British emigrant ships on long
voyages. I have read more than' once in the Australian
papers complaints of their treatment on some of these
boats by new arrivals in the Commonwealth.

Las Palmas was our first port of call. Here, as nearly
everywhere we have been on this tour, one of the out-
standing features is the much greator amount of sun-
shine one finds than in the United Kingdom. The
view from the heights over Las Palmas is good. T took
-a drive with some friends, one of whom speaks Spanish.
We wanted to see bananas growing, and the driver was
instructed accordingly. He drove us to the fruit-
market in the heart of the town!

Our stay at Cape Town was short. We visited tho
Union Parliament, and were shown round by some of
the Dutch members, who spoke in high praise of the
working of self-government in South Africa. Before we
got to the Cape, a third-class passenger dreamt a dream
on three successive nights. His dream was that our
good boat would never reach Tasmania! He was so
much influenced by this that he would not come beyond
the Cape with us. A few nights later we got our first real
tossing about, and some of those on board began to
think of the man we left behind us—and his dreams.
But it was nothing to spealk of. We met with no
storms from start to finish of the passage.

The albatross are to be seen in great numbers after
leaving the Cape. They fly over and around the ship,

_and seem to follow all day in its wake. They are
glorious birds, many of them being twelve feet from tip
to tip of their wings. Sometimes sailors catch them.
Once on deck they are unable to attain flight and get
very seasick. The flying fish, also, are interesting.
We saw few whales, unlike the occasion of my journey
to the Argentine, when during three days we must have
seen close upon fifty or sixty of the monsters. The
Southern Lights appeared in the sky on two nights, per-
pendicular rays of varying brilliance; and, of course,
those disappointing stars called the Southern Cross were
pointed out to travellers who had journeyed over ‘‘the
line *’ for the first time.

We had a couple of days at Hobart, and it rained
during most of our stay. The harbour is one of the
beautiful harbours of the world. Australasia is blessed
with many splendid natural harbours, though, on parts
of the continent, needless to say, the accommodation in
this respect is bad. Wellington harbour is pretty,
Auckland is beautiful, Sydney is glorious. But when-
ever now I see a harbour for the first time, my thoughts
go back to Rio de Janerio—in my opinion, the loveliest
and most perfectly beautiful spot in all the world. For
beauty, Rio is worthy to be called the Gates of Paradise.
Just to look upon it, drew tears from my eyes. Even
now, at times, the recollection of its loveliness thrills
me, and produces the effect one gets from certain kinds
of good music. I had not believed the place possible.
When I saw it, I wondered whether it were real. Words-
worth called it the finest site on earth for a capital city.
That could not be disputed. And in recent years, in
the Awenida Central, there has been built there a busi-
ness thoroughfare unequalled in any other capital city
elsewhere.  But it is the *“ setting " of the place and

mally gracious clerk of the weather, Hobart treated us
well. The Mayor and courfcillors, accompanied by the
Acting-Premier and other Ministers, received and wel-
comed us. We were given a banquet in the evening, at
which Archbishop Delaney presided, and attended other
functions thoughtfully provided for us. %

From Hobart to Wellington, the New Zealand capital,
is only four days’ sail. The last days of a long trip,
such as we were concluding, are, I think, always rest-
less, uncomfortable days. People are packing up, writ-
ing hwried letters for which there was no time during
the six weeks’ passage; the crew are busy getting up
the derricks for the discharge of cargo; and, generally,
there is a disturbed atmosphere aboard, quite different
from the lazy serenity that preceded it. I was somry
the journey was over. I had enjoyed it thoroughly.
It was a splendid rest, the company was pleasant, the
officers friendly and obliging. ~ Then, I had got hold
of a few good books. Chess was much in evidence, and
we played a great deal of that. There were, besides,
the usual board-ship amusements—deck games, sports,
concerts, and dances. For most of these, the first and
second class combined ; but I think the third class beat
the combine alike in the quality and quantity of its
musical talent.

We felt in good form as the ‘“ Rotorua’” drew along-
side one of the many splendid wharves in Wellington
Harbour, close to the centre of the town. Passing the
doctor and getting through the customs did not take
long, though both services are strict. One emigrant was
at first refused permission to enter the country because
of ill-health; but, later, he was allowed to land. No
sooner was the gangway oub, than Martin Kennedy
headed a group of friends up its slope to greet us. He
occupies towards the Irish world of New Zealand much
the same position as does Michael J. Ryan towards that
of America. He was the organiser-in-chief of our cam-
paign. He is an elderly man, low-sized, with a white,
pointed beard, steady eye, keen mind, and quiet
manner. Ho is not a public speaker, and, though he
amassed his handsome fortune by hard work in the
early days, he confines his business operations now to
the interests of the several companies of which he is a
director, including the chief bank of the country—the
Bank of New Zealand. . He was invaluable to our work,
taking, as he did, an affectionate interest in its success,
for the sake of Ireland. He thought of little else dur-
ing our stay of three months in the country. We stayed
in his fine house whenever we were in Wellington. In

LEGISLATION AND INDECENT LITERATURE.

AGNIFICENT work has been done by the various
Vigilance Committees throughout the country
in sweeping out the newspaper and periodical that dealt
in dirt, either by the detailed publication of filthy cases,
or by the more dangerous and insidious form of indecent
advertisements. So far, so well.  This is the first step
towards ultimate, em\uring success. There is now
a temporary lull.  After the storm, a calm! after the
fight, rest. We must be up and doing once again. The
crown may be put on the efforts of this brilliant Irish
campaign by the passing, in the present session of Par-
liament, of legislation, which shall make it penal for a
publisher to traffic in vice or grow rich on the corruption
and degradation of his fellows. :

On the 7th December last, the Home Secretary, Mr.
McKenna, received a deputation, headed by Lord Aber-
deen, with reference to the amendment of the Indecent
Advertisement Act, and the introduction of a Bill deal-
ing with the White Slave Traffic. The London Council
for the Promotion of Public Morality, the National
Vigilance Association and twelve other kindred societies
were represented.  Six Anglican Bishops and several
Nonconformist clergymen were among the deputation,
and Lord Aberdeen stated they had the approval and
hearty support of Cardinal Bourne. The case against
indecent literature and indecent advertisements was put
forcibly by the Lord Lieutenant and the Bishop of Lon-
don. Inreply, Mr. McKenna said the Government had
already drafted a Bill which had not yet been intro-
duced, but which they recognised contained highly
contentious matter. The driving force must be public
and Parliamentary opinion. When public opinion was
strong enough Parliament could be forced, and he would
be a very willing instrument in their endeavour to move
Parliament in this direction.

A few days ago, Mr. McKenna informed Sir H. Car-
lisle in Parliament that he proposed to introduce a Bill
this session dealing with improper literature, and he
hoped that time might be found to pass it.

 THE LEADER.

To the deputation, in December, Mr. McKenna said
there was more probability of immediate action being
taken with regard to the White Slave Traffic Bill than
with the amendment of Indecent Advertisements Act,
but yet he could not undertake to introduce the Bill next
session. The answer to H. Carlisle shows a mOB‘t'
distinct advance, ag the ““driving force of public opinion
has made it possible for the Government to introduce &
Bill this present, session.

Government means well on this question and should
be helped in its efforts to legislate. The creation of
public opinion and its expression through the different
public bodies is a matter of immediate and supreme im-
portance. Parliamentary opinion must be formed by
our Irish Parliamentary representatives, in whom we
have every confidence. Mr. O’Malley, who was on the
Seloct Committee on Lotteries and Indecent Advertise-
ments can certainly be relied on to do yeoman's service.

The County and Urban Councils, the Gaelic League,
A.0.H., Land and Labour Associations and vm'igus
workingmen’s organisations can give invaluable assist-
ance by passing resolutions calling for necessary legis-
lation as regards indecent literature, and sending copies
to the Home Secretary, the Lord Lieutenant, Mr. John
Redmond, and their representatives in Parliament. The
Limerick Board of Guardians has already, on the pro-
posal of one of its most progressive members, passed
unanimously such a resolution. The Vigilance Com-
mittees throughout the country have their work cut oub
for them in approaching public boards, councils, and
socicties, and inducing them to give expression to public
opinion, and thereby facilitate the progress of the Bill
which is to be introduced this session, and which shall
be the crown of all their efforts. Immediate action is
absolutely necessary. The Vigilance Committees have
been tried and have shown what stuff they are made of.
They have won all along the line. They have crushed
the wealthy reptile Press, laughed at morbid * intel-
lectuals,”’ lovers of licence, and advocates of filth.
They will not shirk the last effort needful for permanent
success. SARSFIELD.

AGRICULTURAL SCHEMES.

2 I HAVE often wondered that the Agricultural and
Technical Instruction Committees in the differ-
ent counties have not included among their schemes one-
of working what we might call Model Farms. The ob-
ject of the Department is to teach the most improved
methods of farming, or, in other words, to show each
man how to make the most of his farm. And one would
think the most obvious method of doing this should be
to let him see a farm worked and managed according to-
the best advice the experts of the Department can give.

I have heard of only one county—Monaghan, I think
—in which this has been tried, and the audited ac-
counts of the two farms that were managed by the
Agricultural Instructor showed a substantial profit.
But in this case the accounts were not published in suffi-
cient detail to be of much practical vaiue.

The supervision of such a farm should occupy little:
of any instructor’s time, and could hardly interfere
with his other duties. And it is hard to imagine how
he could do a greater service to his employers than by
showing a farm of average size and quality to those in
the locality but in which the most approved methods
were employed.

And the scheme would entail very little cost. The
County Committee should procure the farm and provide
sufficient capital in the shape of farm stock, imple-
ments, etc. If a capable man was put in charge to
worls it under the direction of the instructor, we may
take it that, after paying all expenses, there would be
a balance that should go a long way towards paying in-
terest on the capital required.

An account of a yea working of such a farm
would be most interesting and instructive. It
would show how the farm was left out, the labour ex-
pended on each crop, with cost of manures and seeds,
and how much each crop yielded and how it was dis-
posed of—whether sold or used in feeding stock. Then
it would show the stock, the rations of each kind and
cost of feeding, and how each paid for its keep. *

A simple and plain set of accounts would be an edu-
cation to many, even if they learned nothing else from
the farm. They could see not only what was made in
circumstances similar to their own, but how the result
was brought about. There would be no more valuable
part of the instructor’s annual report than that dealing
with the working of the farm. 3

‘A number of these farms in different counties would
give a decisive answer to an oft-repeated question:
Does tillage pay?

The value of agricultural labour, and the wages a
labourer might reasonably expect and which a farmer
could afford to give, are questions upon which we might
expect this experiment to throw some light.

Experiments are carried out every year to test the
